transcript.text: I don’t know if it’s okay to say this, but I keep staring at my own face in photos and it feels like everyone is judging me. I also had a bad night of sleep because I kept thinking about the deadline at work. It’s exhausting trying to juggle all of that at once.

You're feeling really overwhelmed, like you're under a microscope, and it's exhausting trying to meet all these expectations - both at work and with how you see yourself. It sounds like the pressure to be perfect is really getting to you, and it's affecting your sleep and your self-perception. Yeah, it can be really tough to quiet that inner critic, especially when you're already feeling anxious about work deadlines. Can you tell me more about what's been going through your mind when you look at those photos?

I guess when I see the photos, I keep staring at my ears and it feels like everyone is judging me; I can't help but think they’re too big. It makes me anxious and I keep worrying about how others will react.

You're getting caught up in some pretty harsh self-criticism when you look at those photos, and it's like your ears become this focal point for all that anxiety. It's exhausting to feel like you're under a microscope, and it sounds like that's really affecting your self-perception. Yeah, it can be really tough to quiet that inner critic, especially when you're already feeling anxious about how others might react.

I’m not sure I can keep doing this, the deadline is almost here and I keep checking my phone for every notification. I can’t stop staring at my ears in the photos, and it just makes everything feel worse. I keep thinking I’m stuck and I’m not sure how to get out of this loop.

You're feeling really stuck right now, like you're caught in this loop of anxiety and self-criticism. It's like everything is piling up, from the deadline to your worries about how you look, and it's hard to know how to escape. Yeah, it can be really tough to quiet that inner critic, especially when you're already feeling overwhelmed. What's one thing that you think might help you take a step back and breathe, even if it's just for a moment?

I could try closing my eyes for a minute, but I'm not sure if that'll help with the deadline and the photos. I keep checking my phone, so maybe it won't stop me from procrastinating. I don't know if this is a good idea.

You're not sure if closing your eyes for a minute will help, and that's okay - it's normal to have doubts when we're trying something new. It sounds like you're feeling a bit stuck, and you're not sure what will help you move forward. Yeah, it can be tough to quiet that inner critic, especially when you're already feeling anxious about the deadline and how you look in photos. What do you think is the smallest step you could take right now to start feeling a bit more in control?
Maybe I could set a 10‑minute timer to just start, but I'm not sure it'll move me forward.

You're considering setting a 10-minute timer to get started, and that takes a lot of courage, especially when you're feeling uncertain about whether it'll make a difference. It's like you're trying to find a way to break through the anxiety and self-doubt that's been holding you back. Yeah, it can be really tough to quiet that inner critic, but it sounds like you're willing to try something new, and that's a great first step. What do you think might happen if you do set that timer and just start, even if it's just for those 10 minutes?

I’m not sure this will do anything; I keep checking my phone and feel like I’m just stalling. Even if I start, I keep looking at my ears in the photos and it’s hard to focus. I also haven’t slept well and that’s just adding to the pressure at work.

You're feeling really overwhelmed, like you're under a lot of pressure to perform at work and look a certain way, and it's hard to focus on anything else. It takes a lot of courage to admit when we're feeling stuck, and it sounds like you're trying to find ways to break through that anxiety and self-doubt. Yeah, it can be tough to quiet that inner critic, especially when you're already feeling anxious about work deadlines and how you look in photos. One thing that might help is to take a step back and try to focus on the present moment, rather than getting caught up in worries about the future or past. Would setting that 10-minute timer and just starting on your task help you feel more in control, even if it's just for a little while?
Maybe I could try the 10‑minute timer, but I'm not sure it'll help. I keep checking my phone and still see my ears in the photos, which makes it hard to focus.

It can be really tough to quiet that inner critic, especially when you're already feeling anxious about how you look in photos. It sounds like you're trying to find ways to break through that anxiety and self-doubt, and it takes a lot of courage to admit when we're feeling stuck. Maybe trying that 10-minute timer could be a small step towards feeling more in control, even if it's just for a little while. What do you think might happen if you do set that timer and just start, even if it's just for those 10 minutes?
I’m not sure the timer will help—my phone keeps pulling me back in, and I keep staring at my ears in the photos. The deadline’s still looming and I can’t sleep well. I just feel stuck.

You're feeling stuck, and it's like everything is pulling you in different directions - your phone, the photos, the deadline, and lack of sleep. It's really tough to focus when all these things are competing for your attention. Your anxiety and self-criticism are making it even harder to quiet your mind and take that first step. Yeah, it's okay to feel overwhelmed. What's the smallest part of this situation that you feel like you could tackle, even if it's just for a minute?
Maybe I could just close my phone for 5 minutes, but I'm not sure it will help with the deadline or the photos. I also worry my ears will still be on my mind.

You're thinking about closing your phone for 5 minutes, which could be a tiny step towards taking control. It's interesting that you're considering this, given how much your phone has been pulling you in. What do you think might be the most challenging part of keeping your phone closed for those 5 minutes?

The hardest part is that my brain keeps jumping to the next notification, and I feel like I'm missing out. I keep worrying about the deadline and how people will see my ears, so the phone feels like a distraction. I just can't stop checking it.

It can be really tough to quiet your mind when you're feeling anxious about the deadline and how you look in photos. Yeah, it's like your brain is constantly jumping to the next thing, and it's hard to focus. I've noticed that you've been trying to find ways to break through that anxiety, like considering closing your phone for 5 minutes. What do you think would happen if you could find a way to silence that inner critic, even if it's just for a little while?

turn_traces.user_message: I don’t know if it’s okay to say this, but I keep staring at my own face in photos and it feels like everyone is judging me. I also had a bad night of sleep because I kept thinking about the deadline at work. It’s exhausting trying to juggle all of that at once.
